 {"id":"wa-2mwk","title":"Circuit breaker infrastructure: state machine, cooldowns, and status reporting","description":"
# Circuit Breaker Infrastructure

## Purpose
Implement a generic circuit breaker pattern to prevent retry storms when services fail.

## State Machine
```
     failures < threshold
CLOSED ◄────────────────────────► OPEN
  │                                  │
  │ failure >= threshold             │ cooldown elapsed
  └──────────────────►               ▼
                              HALF-OPEN
                                  │
                    success → CLOSED
                    failure → OPEN
```

## Implementation
```rust
pub struct CircuitBreaker {
    name: String,
    state: CircuitState,
    failure_count: u32,
    failure_threshold: u32,
    cooldown: Duration,
    last_failure: Option<Instant>,
}

impl CircuitBreaker {
    pub fn call<T>(&self, f: impl FnOnce() -> Result<T>) -> Result<T, CircuitError>;
    pub fn record_success(&mut self);
    pub fn record_failure(&mut self, error: &Error);
    pub fn status(&self) -> CircuitStatus;
}
```

## Circuit Breakers to Implement
- browser_auth: For Playwright auth flows
- caut_cli: For account usage queries
- wezterm_cli: For pane operations
- webhook: For notification delivery

## Status Reporting
```
$ wa doctor --circuits

Circuit Breaker Status:
  browser_auth: CLOSED (healthy)
  caut_cli:     OPEN (3 failures, retry in 4m32s)
  wezterm_cli:  CLOSED (healthy)
  webhook:      HALF-OPEN (testing...)
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] CircuitBreaker struct with state machine
- [ ] Configurable thresholds and cooldowns
- [ ] Status visible in wa doctor
- [ ] Structured logging on state changes

## Testing
- Unit tests for breaker/backoff logic and state transitions.
- Integration tests simulating IO failures and recovery paths.
- Chaos harness scenarios with verbose logs and recovery assertions.

 {"id":"wa-2ojrv","title":"Write integration tests for full asupersync stack","description":"# Write integration tests for full asupersync stack

## Goal
End-to-end tests exercising the complete FrankenTerm system on asupersync. These are the final validation before removing tokio.

## Test scenarios (each is a separate test function)

### 1. Full lifecycle (seed: 100)
Start runtime → create MuxPool → acquire connection → execute mux command → release → shutdown
Log: runtime init, pool create, acquire attempt/success, command send/recv, release, shutdown sequence

### 2. Concurrent pool operations (seed: 200)
Spawn 20 tasks, each acquiring from pool (max_size=5), executing command, releasing.
Log: per-task acquire/release, pool stats at each step, concurrency level over time

### 3. IPC round-trip (seed: 300)
Start IPC server → CLI client connects → sends JSON command → daemon processes → response returned → client disconnects
Log: server accept, request parse, command dispatch, response serialize, client disconnect

### 4. Event streaming (seed: 400)
Start native event listener → mock WezTerm sends events → verify channel receives all events in order
Log: event emit, channel reserve/commit, event receive, ordering verification

### 5. Mid-operation cancellation (seed: 500)
Start long-running operation (pane subscription) → cancel after 3 events → verify clean shutdown
Log: subscription start, each delta received, cancellation trigger, scope drain, obligation check

### 6. Timeout cascade (seed: 600)
Set tight Budget::deadline → operations that exceed deadline → verify proper Cancelled propagation through call stack
Log: budget set, operation start, deadline trigger, Cancelled propagation through each scope level

### 7. Connection recovery (seed: 700)
Acquire connection → simulate broken pipe → next acquire creates new connection → verify transparent recovery
Log: first acquire, pipe break injection, error detection, new connection create, recovery success

### 8. Full system chaos test (seed: 800, chaos enabled)
Full lifecycle with random failure injection: I/O errors, timeouts, channel closures.
Log: injection points, error handling paths taken, recovery actions, final state verification

## Logging format
All integration tests use structured JSON logging with:
```json
{
  "timestamp": "virtual_or_real",
  "test": "test_name",
  "seed": 123,
  "scope": "scope_id",
  "event": "acquire_start|acquire_ok|send_pdu|recv_pdu|...",
  "details": { ... }
}
```

## Acceptance criteria
- All 8 test scenarios pass
- Each test has detailed structured JSON logging
- Logs sufficient to diagnose any failure from log output alone
- No obligation leaks (oracle check in every test)
- No task leaks (oracle check in every test)
- Tests reproducible via seed
- Chaos test exercises at least 5 different failure injection points

## LabRuntime DPOR
- **Integration test determinism with DPOR**: Use LabRuntime schedule exploration (DPOR) for the concurrent test scenarios (scenarios 2, 4, 5, 7) to ensure correctness under all relevant interleavings. For each DPOR-explored scenario:
  - Set max_schedules based on task count (e.g., 100 schedules for 5-task scenarios, 1000 for 20-task)
  - Verify all oracle checks pass under every explored schedule
  - Log the total number of schedules explored and any schedule that triggered a new code path

## Benchmark requirements
- **Criterion benchmarks for stack throughput**: Add `benches/integration_throughput.rs` measuring:
  - Full lifecycle throughput: complete cycles/sec (create pool → acquire → command → release)
  - Concurrent pool throughput: aggregate ops/sec with N=5,10,20 concurrent tasks
  - IPC round-trip latency under load (1, 10, 100 concurrent clients)
  - Event streaming throughput: events/sec through the full pipeline (emit → channel → consume)
  - End-to-end latency percentiles (p50, p95, p99) for representative workloads","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-10T03:51:12.522797Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T19:52:02.707091Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-2ojrv","depends_on_id":"wa-22x4r","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T03:52:03.718609Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"},{"issue_id":"wa-2ojrv","depends_on_id":"wa-181uk","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T03:52:03.911647Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"}]}
 {"id":"wa-2oph2","title":"SIMD-accelerated pane output processing — vectorized scanning and compression","description":"# SIMD-Accelerated Output Processing

## Skills: /alien-artifact-coding, /extreme-software-optimization

## Problem
200 panes producing output simultaneously. Each pane output must be scanned for patterns, compressed for storage, and forwarded to observers. At scale, this is CPU-bound.

## Solution: Vectorized processing pipeline

### 1. SIMD pattern scanning
Use SIMD (AVX2/NEON) to scan pane output for:
- Newline boundaries (for line counting)
- ANSI escape sequence detection (for stripping)
- Pattern triggers (e.g., error keywords, completion markers)
- UTF-8 validation

Reference: /alien-artifact-coding for formal correctness proof of SIMD scanning.

### 2. Vectorized compression
- Use zstd with SIMD acceleration for scrollback compression
- Batch multiple pane outputs into single compression calls
- Pre-trained dictionary for terminal output (high compression ratio)
- **Plugs into wa-2ahu0 memory engine**: provides the compression primitives that the memory pressure engine calls for scrollback compression/eviction

### 3. Zero-copy output pipeline
- mmap-based buffer for pane output capture
- Process in-place without allocation
- Only copy when observer needs a snapshot

### 4. Benchmarks (/extreme-software-optimization)
Must profile and prove:
- Pattern scan throughput: >2GB/s per core
- Compression throughput: >500MB/s per core
- Zero-copy reduces allocation by >90% vs current

## Tests
- **Criterion benchmarks** for pattern scanning (varying output patterns: plain text, ANSI-heavy, binary-heavy)
- **Criterion benchmarks** for compression (terminal output corpus with pre-trained dictionary)
- Throughput test: 200 panes producing 10MB/s each, verify no backpressure
- SIMD correctness: identical results to scalar fallback on ALL inputs (property-based testing with proptest)
- **Platform tests**: AVX2 on x86_64, NEON on aarch64 (Apple Silicon), scalar fallback everywhere

## Acceptance criteria
- SIMD scanning >2GB/s throughput
- Compression >500MB/s throughput with >5:1 ratio on terminal output
- Zero-copy pipeline reduces allocations measurably
- Scalar fallback for non-SIMD platforms
- Formally verified correctness (/alien-artifact-coding)

## Test Framework Requirements
- **Criterion benchmarks**: Must demonstrate >4x improvement over scalar baseline for all SIMD paths:
  - Newline scanning: benchmark SIMD vs scalar on 1KB, 64KB, 1MB, 16MB buffers. SIMD must achieve >4x speedup.
  - ANSI escape detection: benchmark on ANSI-heavy terminal output (50%+ escape sequences). SIMD must achieve >4x speedup.
  - UTF-8 validation: benchmark against std::str::from_utf8 baseline. SIMD must achieve >4x speedup on valid UTF-8 and equivalent error detection on invalid.
  - Compression: benchmark zstd with and without SIMD. Measure both throughput and compression ratio.
- **Proptest for SIMD/scalar equivalence**: For every SIMD-accelerated function, generate random byte sequences and verify the SIMD result is bit-identical to the scalar fallback:
  - Newline positions must match exactly
  - ANSI escape boundaries must match exactly
  - UTF-8 validation accept/reject decisions must match exactly
  - Compression/decompression roundtrip must produce identical output

## macOS Compatibility (Apple Silicon)
- **NEON on Apple Silicon**: All Apple Silicon Macs (M1/M2/M3/M4) support NEON SIMD. Use `std::arch::aarch64` intrinsics with `#[cfg(target_arch = "aarch64")]`.
- **Test both AVX2 and NEON paths**: CI must test AVX2 on x86_64 Linux/macOS and NEON on aarch64 macOS. Use runtime feature detection (`is_x86_feature_detected!("avx2")`) on x86_64 and compile-time `#[cfg(target_arch = "aarch64")]` for NEON (always available on Apple Silicon).
- **Scalar fallback**: Always available for platforms with neither AVX2 nor NEON. The scalar path is the reference implementation for correctness testing.
- **Benchmark all three paths**: Criterion benchmarks should include NEON results on Apple Silicon alongside AVX2 results on x86_64, with scalar as the baseline for both.

## Cross-References
- **wa-3bja.3** (Ghostty I/O pipeline): Ghostty achieves extreme I/O performance using SIMD-accelerated terminal parsing in Zig. This bead applies the same philosophy in Rust — study Ghostty's approach to vectorized escape sequence parsing and adapt the techniques. The two beads share performance targets and may share benchmark infrastructure.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-02-10T16:12:09.705391Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-10T19:47:03.879984Z"}

 {"id":"wa-2pir","title":"Web server tests: /health + basic endpoint contract (feature web)","description":"# Task: Web server tests (feature web)

## Goal
Prevent the optional web server from silently rotting.

The web server is not on the critical path, but when enabled it must be reliable, safe, and schema-stable.

## Test scope
- Runs under `cargo test --features web`.
- Fully offline (no real WezTerm required).

## Required test cases
- Lifecycle:
  - start server on `--port 0`
  - hit `/health`
  - shut down cleanly

- Contract:
  - `/health` schema is parseable and stable
  - `/panes`, `/events`, `/search` responses are:
    - valid JSON
    - stable ordering where applicable
    - redacted (no raw secrets)

- Safety:
  - default bind is localhost
  - public bind requires explicit `--dangerous-bind-any`

## Observability in tests
- Capture request logs/spans and assert a few key fields exist (method/path/status/latency).
- Assert that sensitive values do not appear in logs.

## Acceptance Criteria
- `cargo test --features web` includes at least:
  - `/health` responds
  - endpoint response bodies are schema-parseable
  - redaction + bind-default invariants are enforced


## Testing
- Meta-validation:
  - Add a negative test that attempts `--dangerous-bind-any` without explicit confirmation (if applicable) and ensure it is rejected.
  - Add an assertion that there are no write/mutation endpoints registered.

 {"id":"wa-d23s","title":"Add wa robot approve command","description":"# wa robot approve

## Current State (CLI Stub Done)
✅ CLI subcommand structure added
✅ JSON schema created (wa-robot-approve.json)
❌ Implementation returns not-implemented error

## What Remains
Integrate with ApprovalStore to validate and consume tokens.

## Implementation
1. Accept approval code parameter
2. Query ApprovalStore for token
3. Validate workspace scope, expiry, fingerprint
4. Mark as consumed on success
5. Record in audit trail

## Testing Requirements
- Unit: Valid code consumes token and returns success
- Unit: Expired codes return E_APPROVAL_EXPIRED
- Unit: Already-consumed codes return E_APPROVAL_CONSUMED
- Unit: Wrong pane/workspace returns appropriate error
- E2E: Full approval flow (request → grant → consume)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Validates approval codes via ApprovalStore
- [ ] Consumes token on success
- [ ] All error cases return stable error codes
- [ ] Audit trail records approval consumption
- [ ] JSON validates against schema
- [ ] Unit + E2E tests pass with detailed logging

Related: wa-43rm (detailed implementation spec)
Blocked by: wa-4vx.8.9 (approval token infrastructure)","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-22T18:21:36.255366399Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-11T00:47:48.281194-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-22T19:43:18.249089584Z","close_reason":"Duplicate of bd-39wn which is more detailed (has error codes, pane context, relationship to human command)","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-d23s","depends_on_id":"wa-43rm","type":"relates-to","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:22Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-d23s","depends_on_id":"wa-d2z6","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:22Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-d23s","depends_on_id":"wa-4vx.8.9","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:22Z","created_by":"import"}]}
 {"id":"wa-d248l","title":"Phase 3c: Rename feature flags vendored→frankenterm","description":"Rename feature flags in wa-core and wa Cargo.toml files:

## wa-core/Cargo.toml changes
- New: frankenterm = ["frankenterm-deps"]
- New: frankenterm-deps = ["dep:codec", "dep:config", "dep:mux", "dep:wezterm-term"]
- Changed: vendored = ["frankenterm"] (backward compat alias)
- Changed: vendored-wezterm = ["frankenterm-deps"] (backward compat alias)

## wa/Cargo.toml changes
- New: frankenterm = ["wa-core/frankenterm"]
- Changed: vendored = ["frankenterm"] (backward compat alias)

## Rationale
The name "vendored" no longer applies — we own the code. "frankenterm" signals the project's philosophy of combining best pieces from multiple sources. Backward compat aliases ensure existing CI/scripts/muscle-memory continue to work.

 {"id":"wa-d57z","title":"Design: prepare/commit UX + plan-hash binding semantics","description":"# Task: Design prepare/commit approvals

## Goal
Specify the prepare/commit UX and the exact semantics of plan-hash binding.

## Requirements
- UX flows:
  - human CLI: prepare shows plan + hash, commit executes
  - robot: returns structured prepare result with remediation
  - MCP: same contract as robot
- Binding semantics:
  - approvals bind to:
    - workspace
    - plan_hash
    - action kind(s)
    - target pane_uuid (when applicable)
    - TTL
  - commit must refuse execution if:
    - plan hash mismatch
    - TTL expired
    - target pane identity changed
- Errors:
  - stable error codes and actionable remediation

## Acceptance Criteria
- The design prevents TOCTOU and confused-deputy approval mistakes.

Audit logs exist but are hard to consume in real time or integrate with external tooling.

## Goals
- Provide a streaming/tailing interface for audit logs
- Offer JSONL output for external ingestion (SIEM/log shipper)

## Non-Goals

## Considerations
- Streaming must not block writer threads
- Output must remain data-only (no ANSI)

- Unit + e2e tests validate streaming and redaction","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-02-01T03:17:42.40508359Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-11T00:47:48.317616-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-07T22:19:13.430195857Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-d8d1","depends_on_id":"wa-5em.6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:22Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-d8d1","depends_on_id":"wa-nu4.3.2.9","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:22Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-d8d1","depends_on_id":"wa-05ca","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T00:22:31Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-d8d1","depends_on_id":"wa-9oy1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T00:22:31Z","created_by":"import"}]}

 {"id":"wa-g4xb","title":"Docs: timing determinism guidelines (no sleeps; quiescence patterns)","description":"# Task: Document timing determinism guidelines

## Goal
Prevent flakiness from re-entering the codebase by documenting best practices.

## Requirements
- Document:
  - when to use wait-for vs quiescence
  - how to set timeouts and backoff
  - how to write actionable timeout errors
  - explicit "no fixed sleep" rule + exceptions policy

## Acceptance Criteria
- Contributors can follow a short checklist to write deterministic tests.

 {"id":"wa-g7p2","title":"Human CLI tests: output layer snapshots + no-ANSI guarantees","description":"# Task: Human CLI tests for output layer

## Goal
Make the human CLI output layer trustworthy and regression-resistant.

Humans will pipe wa output into files, paste it into issues, and rely on it under stress. Output regressions are user-hostile.

## Scope

### Renderer-level snapshot tests (core renderers)
Renderer-level tests (no subprocess) for the **Phase 4 baseline** human CLI:
- status/panes table renderer
- events list/panel renderer (**raw/technical** event fields)
- query/search results renderer
- workflow summary renderer
- audit feed renderer

### Explicit non-scope (covered elsewhere)
To keep priorities and dependencies sane, this bead intentionally does **not** block on Phase 4 “UX excellence extras”. Those renderers/overlays must still be tested, but they are covered by their own beads:
- Natural-language event descriptions in event rendering: `wa-0go.7`
- History timeline / rollback visualization output: `wa-5em.9`
- Suggestion overlays in status/events/errors: `wa-tp4.8`

This separation keeps the **core CLI output layer** stable early, while allowing optional overlays to evolve without blocking core ship readiness.

## Format selection behavior
- auto mode chooses rich only when explicitly told "tty" (injectable in tests)
- plain mode is stable and contains no ANSI escape sequences
- json mode is stable and uses a documented schema (may share the robot envelope)

## Key requirements
- Snapshot tests for plain output (golden text). The exact formatting should be intentional.
- Snapshot tests for json output (stable fields, stable error codes).
- Redaction expectations:
  - never print secrets in plain/rich output
  - when showing inputs, use a redacted summary consistent with audit rules

## Implementation notes
- Keep renderers pure: typed input -> String.
- Avoid dependence on real TTY detection in tests. Inject a bool or OutputMode instead.

## Acceptance Criteria
- A deliberate formatting change requires updating snapshots.
- Piped output contains no ANSI sequences by default.
- Errors follow the same structure across commands (message + hint + code).

## Testing
- Meta-validation:
  - Add an explicit assertion that plain-mode output contains no `\x1b[` ANSI escapes.
  - Add a fake secret string in inputs and assert it never appears in renderer output.

- Snapshot discipline:
  - Snapshots should be per-renderer and small enough to review; avoid one mega-snapshot that is hard to update.

 {"id":"wa-ggw9w","title":"Make Lua optional in FrankenTerm config crate (feature-flag)","description":"# Make Lua Optional in FrankenTerm Config Crate

## Context
The config crate (frankenterm/config/) hard-depends on mlua with features=[vendored, lua54, async, send, serialize]. Under the new dual-runtime strategy (Option C), Lua becomes optional — users can choose Lua, WASM, or both.

## What changes
1. Make mlua dependency optional behind `lua` feature
2. Make luahelper dependency optional behind `lua` feature  
3. Gate all Lua-related code in config/src/ behind `#[cfg(feature = "lua")]`
4. Default features INCLUDE `lua` for backward compat
5. `no-lua` feature excludes it (for minimal/headless builds)
6. Ensure config crate compiles and provides basic config loading even without Lua

## Feature flag design
```toml
[features]
default = ["lua"]
lua = ["dep:mlua", "dep:luahelper"]
no-lua = []  # Explicit opt-out
```

## Files affected
- frankenterm/config/Cargo.toml (feature flags, optional deps)
- frankenterm/config/src/lua.rs (gate behind #[cfg(feature = "lua")])
- frankenterm/config/src/lib.rs (conditional module inclusion)
- Any file in config/src/ that imports from lua.rs

## Why keep Lua as default
Existing WezTerm users have wezterm.lua configs. Breaking them by default would be hostile. The `lua` feature is default-on, `no-lua` is opt-in for minimal builds.

## Testing
- `cargo check -p config` — must work (default, with Lua)
- `cargo check -p config --no-default-features` — must compile (no Lua)
- `cargo check -p config --features lua` — explicit Lua, must work
- `cargo test -p config` — existing tests pass

## Cross-References
- **wa-3k2g1** (luahelper optional): Once config gates its luahelper dep behind `lua`, the luahelper crate becomes truly optional at the workspace level. This bead is a prerequisite for wa-3k2g1.
- **wa-2ufom** (Mux Lua optional): The mux crate depends on config. Mux's Lua optionality depends on config's Lua optionality being done first — otherwise mux inherits a transitive hard dependency on mlua through config.
- Together with wa-3k2g1 and wa-2ufom, this forms the "Lua-optional triad" — all three must land for a clean no-Lua build path.","notes":"Landed on origin/main (commit 54226a1). Added lua feature (default-on) + optional mlua/luahelper deps; cfg-gated Lua modules + conversion macros; added no-lua stubs so --no-default-features builds. Verified: cargo check -p config --all-targets; cargo check -p config --all-targets --no-default-features; cargo test (workspace) passes with TMPDIR=/Users/jemanuel/tmp-ft. cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings currently fails in multiple workspace crates (pre-existing).","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"PinkElk","created_at":"2026-02-10T06:52:47.712922Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-02-11T01:58:11.886079-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-11T01:58:11.886083-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-ggw9w","depends_on_id":"wa-3dfxb","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T06:52:56.164581Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"},{"issue_id":"wa-ggw9w","depends_on_id":"wa-2i2vi","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T06:52:56.294002Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}"}]}
 {"id":"wa-gq4y","title":"E2E: intentional watcher crash produces crash bundle and triage signals","description":"# Task: E2E crash bundle scenario

## Goal
Prove end-to-end that a watcher crash produces artifacts and shows up in UX surfaces.

## Scenario
- Start watcher.
- Trigger an intentional crash in a controlled test mode.
- Verify:
  - crash bundle is written
  - `wa triage` / `wa doctor` surfaces the crash
  - `wa reproduce` can package it

## Requirements
- No destructive cleanup.
- Artifacts retained on failure.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Crash scenarios are diagnosable via artifacts.

 {"id":"wa-o1bt","title":"E2E: risk-based gating (low-risk allow, medium require-approval)","description":"# Task: E2E risk-based gating

## Goal
Validate risk-scored policy behavior end-to-end.

## Scenario
- Create a low-risk action and ensure it is allowed.
- Create a medium-risk action and ensure it yields RequireApproval with risk summary.
- Optionally, approve and retry to ensure allow-once works with risk metadata.

## Requirements
- No fixed sleeps; use wait-for/quiescence.
- Artifacts include:
  - policy decision JSON (with risk)
  - logs showing gating behavior

## Acceptance Criteria
- E2E demonstrates that risk scoring impacts gating and is visible to users.

 {"id":"wa-o5zh","title":"CLI polish tests: completions, aliases, verbosity, help validation","description":"# CLI polish tests (completions, aliases, verbosity, help)

## Goal
Ensure CLI polish features work correctly and consistently, and remain regression-resistant as new commands are added.

## Test categories

### 1) Completion tests
- Static completions generated for all shells.
- Dynamic completions return correct pane IDs and workflow names.
- No duplicate or missing completions.

### 2) Alias tests
- Built-in aliases expand correctly.
- User aliases load from config.
- Alias conflicts detected.
- `wa aliases` output is accurate.

### 3) Verbosity + format tests
- Default output matches expected format.
- `-v` adds expected additional info.
- `-vv` adds debug-level info.
- `--format json` produces valid, schema-stable JSON.

### 4) Help validation
- All commands have help text.
- All commands have examples.
- Cross-references point to real commands.
- No obvious typos in help text.

### 5) Output consistency
- No ANSI when stdout is not a TTY.
- Exit codes are correct (0 success, 1 error).
- Error messages include remediation.
- JSON envelope is consistent across commands.

## Snapshot/contract tests
- Help text snapshots for regression (normalized).
- Status output snapshots at each verbosity level (normalized; stable ordering).

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Completion tests for all shells.
- [ ] Alias expansion tests.
- [ ] Verbosity/format tests.
- [ ] Help text validation in CI.
- [ ] Output consistency verified.

## Testing
- Unit: completion generation + alias expansion + verbosity routing.
- Integration: help output snapshots and no-ANSI guarantees.
- Schema: JSON output validation for `--format json`.
- CI: smoke tests ensure completions and help remain in sync.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-18T17:55:45.496125952Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-02-11T00:47:48.220959-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-29T05:26:40.493278418Z"}

 {"id":"wa-o8j","title":"WAL recovery: automatic checkpoint and journal cleanup on startup","description":"# Task: WAL Recovery on Startup

## Goal
Handle unclean shutdown scenarios gracefully by recovering WAL/journal state.

## Problem
If wa crashes or is killed while writing:
- WAL file may be large (uncommitted transactions)
- Journal file may exist (transaction in progress)
- Next startup should recover cleanly

## Implementation

### Startup Recovery Sequence
```rust
impl StorageHandle {
    pub fn open_with_recovery(path: &Path) -> Result<Self> {
        // 1. Check for recovery situation
        let wal_path = path.with_extension("sqlite-wal");
        let journal_path = path.with_extension("sqlite-journal");
        
        if wal_path.exists() || journal_path.exists() {
            tracing::info\!(
                wal_exists = wal_path.exists(),
                journal_exists = journal_path.exists(),
                "Recovery situation detected, will attempt recovery"
            );
        }
        
        // 2. Open with recovery pragmas
        let conn = Connection::open(path)?;
        
        // 3. Run quick integrity check
        let result: String = conn.query_row(
            "PRAGMA quick_check",
            [],
            |row| row.get(0)
        )?;
        
        if result \!= "ok" {
            tracing::error\!(result = %result, "Database corruption detected");
            return Err(StorageError::Corruption { details: result });
        }
        
        // 4. Checkpoint WAL if large
        let (busy, log, ckpt): (i32, i32, i32) = conn.query_row(
            "PRAGMA wal_checkpoint(PASSIVE)",
            [],
            |row| Ok((row.get(0)?, row.get(1)?, row.get(2)?))
        )?;
        
        tracing::info\!(
            busy = busy,
            wal_frames = log,
            checkpointed = ckpt,
            "WAL checkpoint completed"
        );
        
        // 5. If WAL is huge, do full checkpoint
        if log > 10000 {
            tracing::warn\!(frames = log, "Large WAL detected, doing full checkpoint");
            conn.execute("PRAGMA wal_checkpoint(TRUNCATE)", [])?;
        }
        
        Ok(Self { conn })
    }
}
```

### Logging Contract
On recovery:
```
INFO  wa::storage: Recovery situation detected wal_exists=true journal_exists=false
INFO  wa::storage: Quick integrity check passed
INFO  wa::storage: WAL checkpoint completed busy=0 wal_frames=5432 checkpointed=5432
INFO  wa::storage: Database recovery complete
```

On corruption:
```
ERROR wa::storage: Database corruption detected result="malformed disk image"
ERROR wa::storage: Cannot recover automatically, run: wa db repair
```

## Testing
- Unit tests: mock WAL/journal scenarios
- Integration: kill wa mid-write, verify recovery on restart
- E2E: wa-4vx.10.12 (workflow resume after restart) covers this path

## Acceptance Criteria
- Unclean shutdown followed by restart recovers automatically
- Large WAL files are checkpointed on startup
- Corruption detected and surfaced clearly
- Recovery actions logged for debugging

 {"id":"wa-oacw","title":"TUI events feed: recent/unhandled, severity colors, select to view details","description":"# Task: TUI events feed

## Goal
Provide an interactive event feed that makes it trivial to:
- see **what wa thinks is happening**
- quickly spot **unhandled / high-severity** events
- drill into why a rule matched (pack/rule/trace)

This is the “incident timeline” view.

## Data contract
Back the view via the same model as `wa robot events`:
- event id
- timestamp
- pane id + domain
- severity
- handled/unhandled status
- rule/pack identifiers
- short redacted summary
- optional match trace (what text matched, in redacted form)

## UX requirements
- Feed view:
  - default sort: newest-first
  - toggle: unhandled-only
  - filter by pane id / domain / event type
  - severity coloring (must degrade gracefully for non-color terminals)

- Event details panel:
  - show full structured fields
  - show rule id + pack id
  - show remediation hint / “recommended next action” if present

## Safety
- Never render raw transcript content that could include secrets.
- Always use redacted summaries/traces.

## Testing strategy
- Unit tests (feature `tui`) using fake data:
  - filter toggles work
  - severity sorting is stable
  - details panel renders expected fields
  - redaction is preserved (no accidental display of raw secrets)

## Acceptance Criteria
- User can toggle unhandled-only and immediately see unhandled critical events.
- Selecting an event shows rule/pack ids and a redacted trace/details view.
